source_paper_title: Efficient Approximation Schemes for Stochastic Probing and Selection-Stopping Problems
context:
Section 7 (Future Directions), page 34.

An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time?
title: Existence of an FPTAS for Free-Order Prophets and ProbeMax-type stochastic optimization problems
background_latex:
We consider stochastic combinatorial optimization problems on input size \(|I|\) with objective value \(\mathrm{OPT}(I)\), where the goal is to compute a solution whose expected objective value approximates \(\mathrm{OPT}(I)\). An efficient polynomial-time approximation scheme (EPTAS) is a family of algorithms parameterized by \(\epsilon>0\) that returns a \((1-\epsilon)\)-approximate solution in time \(t(\epsilon)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(|I|)\), where \(t\) depends only on \(\epsilon\). A fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) is a family of algorithms parameterized by \(\epsilon>0\) that returns an \((1-\epsilon)\)-approximate solution in time \(\mathrm{poly}(|I|,1/\epsilon)\). The paper studies (among others) the following maximization problems under independent random variables \(X_1,\dots,X_n\):
- Free-Order Prophets: choose a permutation \(\sigma\in S_n\) and a stopping time \(\tau\) to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[X_{\sigma(\tau)}]\).
- Non-adaptive ProbeMax: choose a set \(S\subseteq [n]\) with \(|S|\le k\) to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\max_{j\in S} X_j]\).
- Adaptive ProbeMax: choose a policy that adaptively probes up to \(k\) variables to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\max\text{ observed } X_j]\).
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether one or more of the stochastic optimization problems studied in this paper (e.g., Free-Order Prophets, non-adaptive ProbeMax, adaptive ProbeMax) admit an FPTAS, i.e., for every \(\epsilon>0\) design an algorithm that outputs a solution of expected value at least \((1-\epsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT}(I)\) and runs in time \(\mathrm{poly}(|I|,1/\epsilon)\).
latex:
We consider stochastic combinatorial optimization problems on input size \(|I|\) with objective value \(\mathrm{OPT}(I)\), where the goal is to compute a solution whose expected objective value approximates \(\mathrm{OPT}(I)\). An efficient polynomial-time approximation scheme (EPTAS) is a family of algorithms parameterized by \(\epsilon>0\) that returns a \((1-\epsilon)\)-approximate solution in time \(t(\epsilon)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(|I|)\), where \(t\) depends only on \(\epsilon\). A fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) is a family of algorithms parameterized by \(\epsilon>0\) that returns an \((1-\epsilon)\)-approximate solution in time \(\mathrm{poly}(|I|,1/\epsilon)\). The paper studies (among others) the following maximization problems under independent random variables \(X_1,\dots,X_n\):
- Free-Order Prophets: choose a permutation \(\sigma\in S_n\) and a stopping time \(\tau\) to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[X_{\sigma(\tau)}]\).
- Non-adaptive ProbeMax: choose a set \(S\subseteq [n]\) with \(|S|\le k\) to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\max_{j\in S} X_j]\).
- Adaptive ProbeMax: choose a policy that adaptively probes up to \(k\) variables to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\max\text{ observed } X_j]\).

Determine whether one or more of the stochastic optimization problems studied in this paper (e.g., Free-Order Prophets, non-adaptive ProbeMax, adaptive ProbeMax) admit an FPTAS, i.e., for every \(\epsilon>0\) design an algorithm that outputs a solution of expected value at least \((1-\epsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT}(I)\) and runs in time \(\mathrm{poly}(|I|,1/\epsilon)\).
open_problem_quote: An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time?
